text.title: ba-governance Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for ba-governance. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Define decision rights before any requirements work begins; document who can approve, who must be consulted, and who must be informed for each decision type.
rule.rationale: Without explicit decision rights, every scope change escalates to the highest available authority, creating steering-committee bottlenecks and delayed delivery.

rule.statement: Every change-control flow must have explicit terminal states: approve, reject with reason, and defer with owner and date; open-ended flows leave CRs in limbo.
rule.rationale: LLM-generated change-control flows frequently lack a "reject + reason" terminal state; unresolved CRs accumulate and make the requirements baseline untrustworthy.

rule.statement: Bind change-control thresholds to objective signals (story points, dollar impact, regulatory flag), not to subjective severity assessments.
rule.rationale: Subjective thresholds drift over time; every CR eventually qualifies as "XL / Steering" because teams learn that higher classification gets faster decisions.

rule.statement: Document tie-breaking rules before any prioritization conflict arises; do not defer tie-breaking to ad-hoc stakeholder negotiation.
rule.rationale: Without explicit tie-breaking rules, priority conflicts become political; the loudest stakeholder wins rather than the most valuable requirement.

rule.statement: Capture every decision including rejections and defers; the audit trail is the deliverable, not the matrix.
rule.rationale: Regulated industries require a complete decision log; even non-regulated projects benefit because rejected CRs contain the reasoning that prevents re-relitigating the same scope question next quarter.

rule.statement: Re-validate the decision-authority matrix every 30 days; stale escalation paths are the leading cause of stuck CRs.
rule.rationale: Org charts change faster than governance documents; a matrix updated at kickoff is obsolete within 3 months on average for growing teams.

rule.statement: Map every communication channel to a feedback mechanism; a channel without a feedback mechanism is write-only and decays within weeks.
rule.rationale: "Email weekly digest" without a reply expectation or read-confirmation mechanism produces a false sense of stakeholder alignment; unresolved misunderstandings surface as surprise issues at sign-off.

rule.statement: Define elicitation scope (what information is needed, what decisions will be made, what is out of scope) before scheduling any session.
rule.rationale: Undefined scope turns sessions into open-ended discussions that produce inconsistent outputs and require rework to extract useful requirements.

rule.statement: Pre-commit the technique-selection table per session; agents otherwise default to "interview" for all information types.
rule.rationale: Each information type has an optimally matched technique; always interviewing leaves process gaps, current-state artifacts, and validation results under-covered.

rule.statement: Require three human-in-the-loop checkpoints: (1) sign-off on the decision-authority matrix before any requirements are baselined; (2) sponsor confirmation of comms cadence; (3) post-kickoff dry-run of the CR flow with one synthetic CR.
rule.rationale: Governance integrity depends on human approval traces at critical junctures; agent-only setup produces a governance document that satisfies process checkers but fails the first real decision.
